elements.sent-2411-001: We address the issue of the testability of instrumental variables derived from observational data.
elements.sent-2411-002: Most existing testable implications are centered on scenarios where the treatment is a discrete variable, e.g., instrumental inequality \citep{pearl1995testability , or where the effect is assumed to be constant, e.g., instrumental variables condition based on the principle of independent mechanisms \citep{burauel2023evaluating .}}
elements.sent-2411-003: However, treatments can often be continuous variables, such as drug dosages or nutritional content levels, and non-constant effects may occur in many real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we consider an additive nonlinear, non-constant effects model with unmeasured confounders, in which treatments can be either discrete or continuous, and propose an Auxiliary-based Independence Test (AIT) condition to test whether a variable is a valid instrument.

elements.sent-2411-007: We also extend the AIT condition to include covariates and introduce a practical testing algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-008: Experimental results on both synthetic and three different real-world datasets show the effectiveness of our proposed condition.
elements.sent-2411-009: Estimating causal effects from observational data is a fundamental task in understanding the underlying relationships between variables.
elements.sent-2411-010: The instrumental variables (IV) model is a well-established method for estimating the causal effect of a treatment (exposure) $X$ on an outcome $Y$ in the presence of latent confounding and has been used in a range of fields, such as economics~\citep{Imbens2014IV,imbens2015causal , sociology \citep{pearl2009causality,spirtes2000causation , epidemiology~\citep{hernan2006instruments,baiocchi2014instrumental , and artificial intelligence \citep{chen2022instrumental,wu2022instrumental .}}}}

elements.sent-2411-013: Due to the presence of unmeasured confounders $\mathbf{U}$, determining which variable serve as a valid IV is not always straightforward based solely on observational data, and often requires domain knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-014: A classic test for IV model is the Durbin-Wu-Hausman test \citep{nakamura1981relationships .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Given a subset of valid IVs, it can identify whether other potential candidates are also valid IVs.

elements.sent-2411-017: Meanwhile, given an invalid IV, it may not consistently identify the correct causal effect ~\citep{bound1995problems,chu2001semi .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Thus, it is vital to develop statistical methods for selecting IVs solely from observational data.
elements.sent-2411-019: It is not feasible to ascertain the validity of IVs solely based on the joint distribution of observed variables, without incorporating additional assumptions \citep{pearl2009causality .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Building on this groundwork, subsequent research by ~\citet{manski2003partial,palmer2011nonparametric,kitagawa2015test,wang2017falsification broadened the scope, exploring the applicability and limitations of IV validity tests across diverse scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-021: A notable advancement was made by \citet{kedagni2020generalized , who formulated a more encompassing set of criteria, the generalized instrumental inequalities.}
elements.sent-2411-022: These criteria cater to scenarios with discrete treatment variables, removing constraints of the type of data on the IV and outcome variables and offering a robust framework to challenge the exogeneity condition.
elements.sent-2411-023: The above methods use the idea that if the IV is independent of the confounders and exclusion restriction ($\mathcal{C 3$) holds, then changes in the IV should not have a significant impact on the outcome variable without altering the treatment variable, because the treatment variable mediates the influence of the instrumental variable on the outcome variable.}
elements.sent-2411-024: However, these methods fail to work when treatment is a continuous variable.
elements.sent-2411-025: In reality, one may often be concerned about the causal effect of the continuous treatment on the outcome; see~\citet{skaaby2013vitamin, martinussen2019instrumental .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Several contributions have been made to address continuous treatment settings under certain assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-027: In an additive linear, constant effects (ALICE) model, \citet{kang2016instrumental,bowden2016consistent , and \cite{windmeijer2019use have shown that if we assume more than half of the variables are valid IVs in the potential IVs (known as the majority rule), we may identify the valid IVs solely from observed data.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Later, \cite{hartwig2017robust,guo2018confidence,windmeijer2021confidence relaxed the majority rule and assumed that the number of valid IVs is larger than any number of invalid IVs with the same ratio estimator limit (known as the plurality rule).}
elements.sent-2411-029: They demonstrated that it is still possible to identify valid IVs under the plurality rule.

elements.sent-2411-032: However, although these methods have been used in a range of fields, they may fail to test whether a single IV is valid.
elements.sent-2411-033: Recently, \citet{xie2022testability have demonstrated that a single IV imposes specific constraints within the linear non-Gaussian acyclic causal model.}
elements.sent-2411-034: However, their method assumes that all noise terms are non-Gaussian and that the effects remain constant.
elements.sent-2411-035: More recently, \citet{burauel2023evaluating have introduced a novel validity condition for Instrumental Variables based on the Principle of Independent Mechanisms, termed IV-PIM, within the linear IV framework.}
elements.sent-2411-036: This condition is particularly notable as it applies to both continuous and discrete treatment variables.

elements.sent-2411-039: Unlike the existing work that focuses on the parametric linear constant effects model, we consider IV validity in a more challenging additive non-parametric model, the Additive NonlInear, Non-Constant Effects (ANINCE) Model.
elements.sent-2411-040: Rather surprisingly, although a single IV is in general not fully testable within the ANINCE model, we will show that a single variable $Z$, being a valid IV relative to $X \rightarrow Y$, imposes specific constraints in the ANINCE model.
elements.sent-2411-041: Specifically, we make the following contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-042: We introduce a necessary condition, termed the Auxiliary-based Independence Test (AIT) condition, for detecting a single variable that cannot serve as an IV within the ANINCE model.
elements.sent-2411-043: This condition is applicable to scenarios involving non-constant causal effects and both discrete and continuous treatment.
elements.sent-2411-044: We further provide the necessary and sufficient conditions for detecting all invalid IVs using the AIT condition under the ANINCE model.
elements.sent-2411-045: Specifically, we show that, under the partial non-Gaussianity assumption (Assumption \ref{Ass-non-Gaussianity-exogeneity ), all observable violations of the IV exogeneity condition can be identified in the linear, constant effects model.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Additionally, under the algebraic equation condition assumption (Assumption \ref{Ass-algebraic-condition ), we can detect invalid IVs resulting from violations of either exogeneity or the exclusion restriction in the ANINCE model.}
elements.sent-2411-047: We also present two notable types of non-identifiable invalid IVs (See Corollaries \ref{Corollary1-Non-testability-IV-ANINCE $\sim$ \ref{Corollary3-Non-testability-IV-ANINCE ), along with intuitive explanations for each.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: We present a practical implementation of the AIT condition test that accounts for the presence of covariates with finite data.
elements.sent-2411-049: We demonstrate the efficacy and applicability of the proposed approach on both synthetic and three real-world datasets with different scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-050: The rest of this paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-051: In Section \ref{Preliminaries , we introduce notations, the additive non-parametric IV model, and the ANINCE model.}
elements.sent-2411-052: In Section \ref{Section-AIT-condition-and-implication , we formulate the AIT condition for the single IV.}
elements.sent-2411-053: We show the AIT condition is a necessary condition for IV validity in the ANINCE model.
elements.sent-2411-054: We discuss the implications of AIT condition in the linear, constant effects model and the nonlinear, non-constant effects model, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-055: We show that, under additional assumptions, the AIT condition is a necessary and sufficient condition for IV validity.
elements.sent-2411-056: In Section \ref{Section-Test-Procedure , we address the practical scenario with covariates and provide the AIT Condition algorithm to test the validity of IV.}
elements.sent-2411-057: We present the efficacy and applicability of our method on both synthetic and three real-world datasets which contain continuous and discrete data in Section \ref{Section:experiments .}

elements.section-2411-003: AIT Condition and Its Implications in ANINCE Models
elements.sent-2411-086: In this section, we first formulate the Auxiliary-based Independence Test Condition {{(AIT Condition) and show that it is a necessary condition for evaluating IV validity.}}

elements.sent-2411-232: In the first stage, it regresses the treatment variable $X$ on covariates $\mathbf{W}$ and instruments $(Z, h_2(Z), \dots, h_k(Z))$ (a known vector of linearly independent functions of $Z$), obtaining the predicted value $\hat{X}$ and the residual $e_1 = X - \hat{X}$.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{AIT Condition} \label{Alg: AIT-Condition-Test} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE Observed dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{X, Y, \mathbf{W}, Z\}$, containing: treatment $X$, outcome $Y$, covariates $\mathbf{W}$, and candidate IV $Z$, and significance level $\alpha$. \STATE Initialize: Result $\gets$ Do not reject $H_0$ /* Step one: Estimate $\mathcal{A}_{X \to Y||(Z, \mathbf{W})}$ */ \IF{there is a constant effect between $X$ and $Y$} \STATE $h(X,\mathbf{W}) \gets \text{Two-Stage Least Squares Estimator }(X, Y, \mathbf{W}, Z)$ \ELSE \STATE $h(X,\mathbf{W}) \gets \text{Control Function IV Estimator }(X, Y, \mathbf{W}, Z)$ \ENDIF \STATE $\mathcal{A}_{X \to Y||(Z,\mathbf{W})} \gets Y - h(X,\mathbf{W})$ /* Step two: Test the AIT condition */ \STATE $\text{Residual } \mathcal{Z} \gets \text{Random Forset Method }(Z,\mathbf{W}); \text{ set }\mathcal{Z} = Z \text{ if } \mathbf{W} = \emptyset$ \STATE $p_{value} \gets \text{Large-Scale HSIC Test}(\mathcal{A}_{X \to Y||(Z,\mathbf{W})}, \mathcal{Z})$ \IF {$p_{value} < \alpha$} \STATE Result $\gets$ Reject $H_0$ \RETURN Result \ELSE \RETURN Result \ENDIF \ENSURE Result \end{algorithmic}

elements.subsubsection-2411-002: Comparison with IV-PIM in Continuous Treatment Setting
elements.sent-2411-273: In this section, we compared the proposed AIT Condition with IV-PIM, as proposed by \citet{burauel2023evaluating , in continuous treatment settings.}
elements.sent-2411-274: Note that since IV-PIM requires covariates, we introduce covariates here for a fair comparison.
elements.sent-2411-275: Experimental Design: The specific generation mechanism with covariates $\mathbf{W}$ in the linear mdoel is defined as follows: $U =\varepsilon_U$, $\mathbf{W = \boldsymbol{\varepsilon_{W }$, $Z_1 =\mathcal{I (U + \mathbf{W + \varepsilon_{Z1 )$, $Z_2= \mathcal{I (\mathbf{W + \varepsilon_{Z2 )$, $X = 0.5Z_1 + 0.5Z_2 + \boldsymbol{\lambda W + \delta$, and $Y = X + \mathbf{W + \epsilon$, where $\varepsilon_{U \sim T(5)$, $\varepsilon_{Z1 \sim Beta(0.5, 0.1)$, $\varepsilon_{Z2 \sim \mathcal{N (0,1)$, and $\delta, \epsilon \sim T(5)$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-276: Here, $\mathcal{I (*)$ is the indicator function such that $\mathcal{I (*) > \text{mean (*)$ equals 1; otherwise, it is 0.}}}
elements.sent-2411-277: The coefficient $\boldsymbol{\lambda}$ is randomly drawn from a normalized standard normal distribution.
elements.sent-2411-278: The noise terms $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon_{W }$ follow a multidimensional normal distribution and are consistent with IV-PIM, with the dimensionality of covariates $\mathbf{W}$ varying across $|\mathbf{W |=\{2, 3, 5\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-279: The remaining settings are the same as in Section \ref{Subsection-simulation-theory .}
elements.sent-2411-280: Results: As shown in Table \ref{Table-IV-PIM-compared-linear-model , our method outperforms experimental results of IV-PIM with covariates under both Valid MR and Invalid Mr. Interestingly, IV-PIM’s performance improves as the dimensionality of covariates increases, consistent with findings in \cite{burauel2023evaluating .}}
elements.sent-2411-281: Additionally, Table \ref{Table-IV-PIM-compared-linear-model highlights the practicality of the AIT condition with covariates, as presented in Corollary \ref{Corollary-Necessary-Condition-IV-covariates .}}

elements.subsubsection-2411-003: Comparison with K-test in Discrete Treatment Setting
elements.sent-2411-282: In this section, we compared the proposed instrument validity test with the K-test, which was proposed by \citet{kitagawa2015test for discrete treatment settings without covariates.}
elements.sent-2411-283: Experimental Design: The discrete treatment data that simulates violations of the exogeneity and exclusion restriction conditions as follows: $U = \varepsilon_{U}$, $Z = \mathcal{I ( \varphi_{Z (U) + \varepsilon_{Z )$, $X = \mathcal{I (g_{X (Z)+ \varphi_{X (U) + \varepsilon_{X )$, $Y = \beta X + g_{Y (Z) + \varphi_{Y (U) + \varepsilon_{Y}$, and $\varepsilon_{* \sim \mathcal{N (0,1)$, where the causal effect $\beta = 1$, and $\mathcal{I (*)$ is the indicator function such that $\mathcal{I (*) > \text{mean (*)$ equals 1; otherwise, it is 0.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-284: The functions $\varphi_{* (U)$ and $g_{* (Z)$ are nonlinear and randomly selected from the following: {cos, sin, square, cubic(third-degree polynomials), logarithmic, exponential function.}}}
elements.sent-2411-285: Results: As shown in Table \ref{Table-binary-treatment-model , our method outperforms the K-test method in terms of Invalid MR across all sample sizes.}
elements.sent-2411-286: The Invalid MR for the K-test method is slightly higher, particularly with smaller sample sizes, though it decreases as the sample size increases.
elements.sent-2411-287: For the Valid MR metric, both methods achieve a value of 0, indicating that neither method mistakenly identifies valid IVs as invalid.

elements.sent-2411-288: In this section, we evaluated the effectiveness of the proposed method by applying it to three real-world datasets from different domains.
elements.sent-2411-289: We consider the application of our method to the study by \citet{card1993using .}
elements.sent-2411-290: This study investigates the impact of education levels on earnings using data from the Young Men Cohort of the National Longitudinal Survey.
elements.sent-2411-291: Data Description: The dataset is a sample of 3010 men taken from the US National Longitudinal Survey of Young Men (NLSY).
elements.sent-2411-292: It includes variables such as Lived near the college ($L_{nc}$), Schooling ($S_{ch}$), Returns ($R_e$), and a set of covariates including \{$\mathbf{E$xperience, Experience square, Black, Smsa, Smsa66, South, $\mathbf{R}$egion information (reg662-reg669)}\} ($\mathbf{ER}$), among others.
elements.sent-2411-293: The hypothesized model of \cite{card1993using is presented in Figure \ref{Fig:real-data-example4 .}}
elements.sent-2411-294: The hypothesized data generation mechanism is described as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} S_{ch} = \, \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 L_{nc} + \boldsymbol{\alpha^{\top}} \mathbf{ER} + \delta, R_e = \, \beta_0 + \beta_1 S_{ch} + \boldsymbol{\beta^{\top}} \mathbf{ER} + \epsilon, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\delta$ and $\epsilon$ are dependent.
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation} S_{ch} = \, \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 L_{nc} + \boldsymbol{\alpha^{\top}} ER + \delta, R_e = \, \beta_0 + \beta_1 S_{ch} + \boldsymbol{\beta^{\top}} ER + \epsilon, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-295: Results: \cite{card1993using demonstrated that $L_{nc}$ can serve as a valid IV for the causal relationship $S_{ch \to R_e$, while controlling for the covariates $\mathbf{ER}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-296: For consistency, we adopt the causal effect of $S_{ch}$ on $R_e$, i.e., $\beta_1 = 0.1315$, as well as the coefficients $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ of the covariates from \cite{card1993using , as the estimated parameter $\hat{\beta}$ under the AIT condition.}
elements.sent-2411-297: We then obtain the residual $\widetilde{L_{nc }$ by regressing $L_{nc}$ on the covariates $\mathbf{ER}$.}
elements.sent-2411-298: The $P$-value of the independence test between the auxiliary variable and the residual $\widetilde{L_{nc }$ is 0.732, indicating that we cannot reject $L_{nc}$ as a valid IV.}
elements.sent-2411-299: This result further supports the validity of using $L_{nc}$ as an IV, consistent with the findings in \cite{card1993using .}
elements.sent-2411-300: We apply our method to the study by \cite{acemoglu2001colonial , which estimates the impact of colonial history on the economic development of different regions using the Colonial Origins of Comparative Development dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-301: Data Description: The dataset includes five key variables across 64 countries, after excluding samples with missing data.
elements.sent-2411-302: These variables are: Mortality ($M_{or}$), Euro1990 ($E_{uro}$), Latitude ($L_{at}$), Institutions ($I_{ns}$), and Economic Development ($E_d$).
elements.sent-2411-303: The hypothesized model proposed by \cite{acemoglu2001colonial is illustrated in Figure \ref{Fig:real-data-example2 , and the hypothesized data generation mechanism is described as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} I_{ns} = \gamma + \gamma_1 M_{or} + \gamma_2 L_{at}+ \gamma_3 E_{uro} + \delta, E_d = \beta + \beta_1 I_{ns} + \beta_2 L_{at} + \epsilon, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\delta$ and $\epsilon$ are dependent.}}
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation} I_{ns} = \gamma + \gamma_1 M_{or} + \gamma_2 L_{at}+ \gamma_3 E_{uro} + \delta, E_d = \beta + \beta_1 I_{ns} + \beta_2 L_{at} + \epsilon, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-304: Results: \cite{acemoglu2001colonial demonstrated that both $M_{or}$ and $E_{uor}$ can serve as valid IVs, conditional on $L_{at}$, with respect to $I_{ns}$ and $E_d$.}
elements.sent-2411-305: To verify this, we test their validity using the AIT condition.
elements.sent-2411-306: For consistency, we adopt the causal effects of $I_{ns}$ on $E_d$ as reported by \cite{acemoglu2001colonial , specifically $\beta_1 = 0.9458$ and $\beta_2 = -0.5971$, as the estimated parameters $\hat{\beta_i (i=1,2)$ in the AIT condition.}}
elements.sent-2411-307: We next obtain the residuals $\widetilde{M_{or }$ and $\widetilde{E_{uro }$ by regressing $M_{or}$ and $E_{uro}$ on covariate $L_{at}$, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-308: The validity test for $M_{or}$ yields a $P$-value of $0.608$, whereas the test for $E_{uro}$ yields a $P$-value of $0.248$.
elements.sent-2411-309: These results indicate that $E_{uro}$ is more likely to be an invalid IV compared to $M_{or}$, suggesting that the exogeneity of $E_{uro}$ is weaker than that of $M_{or}$.
elements.sent-2411-310: These findings are consistent with those of \cite{acemoglu2001colonial , and we cannot reject the validity of $M_{or}$ and $E_{uro}$ as IVs, aligning with their conclusions.}
elements.sent-2411-311: This study uses the Conflict and Time Preference Data to investigate the impact of violence on a person’s patience.
elements.sent-2411-312: Data Description: The dataset consists of 302 observations and fifteen variables, as described in \citet{voors2012violent .}
elements.sent-2411-313: Treatment violence is measured by the percentage dead in attacks in the area the person lived ($D_{per}$), while the person’s patience (outcome) is assessed by a person’s discount rate for willingness to receive larger amounts of money in the future compared to smaller amounts of money now ($D_{isc}$).
elements.sent-2411-314: Other variables include distance to Bujumbura ($d_{ist}$), altitude ($a_{lti}$), and covariates such as \{whether the respondent is literate, the respondent’s age, the respondent’s sex, the total land holding per capita, land Gini coefficient, distance to market, conflict over land, ethnic homogeneity, socioeconomic homogeneity, population density, per capita total expenditure\}($\mathbf{PG}$).
elements.sent-2411-315: The covariates used in the study represent exogenous personal and geographical information variables ($\mathbf{PG}$).
elements.sent-2411-316: As discussed in \citet{voors2012violent , violence may be targeted in a non-random way, potentially related to community patience, which makes violence ($D_{per}$) endogenous.}
elements.sent-2411-317: The hypothesized model from \citet{guo2016control is illustrated in Figure \ref{Fig:real-data-conflict-Preference , and the hypothesized generation mechanism is as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} D_{per} = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 d_{ist} + \alpha_2 a_{lti} + \alpha_3 d_{ist}^{2} + \alpha_4 a_{lti}^{2} + \alpha_5 d_{ist} * a_{lti} + \boldsymbol{\alpha_6^{\top}PG} + \delta, D_{isc} = \beta_0 + \boldsymbol{\beta_1^{\top}PG} + \beta_2 D_{per} + \beta_3 D_{per}^{2} + \epsilon, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\delta$ and $\epsilon$ are dependent.}}
elements.formula-2411-015: \begin{equation} D_{per} = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 d_{ist} + \alpha_2 a_{lti} + \alpha_3 d_{ist}^{2} + \alpha_4 a_{lti}^{2} + \alpha_5 d_{ist} * a_{lti} + \boldsymbol{\alpha_6^{\top}PG} + \delta, D_{isc} = \beta_0 + \boldsymbol{\beta_1^{\top}PG} + \beta_2 D_{per} + \beta_3 D_{per}^{2} + \epsilon, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-318: Results: \citet{voors2012violent showed that both $d_{ist}$ and $a_{lti}$ can serve as valid IVs, conditional on $\mathbf{PG}$, with respect to $D_{per}$ on $D_{isc}$.}
elements.sent-2411-319: For consistency, we adopt the causal effects of $D_{per}$ on $D_{isc}$ as reported by \cite{guo2016control , specifically $\beta_2 = 2.054$ and $\beta_3 = 0.049$, as the estimated parameters $\hat{\beta_i (i=2,3)$ in the AIT condition.}}
elements.sent-2411-320: We obtain the residual $\widetilde{d_{ist }$ and $\widetilde{a_{lti }$ by regressing $d_{ist}$ and $a_{lti}$ on covariates $\mathbf{PG}$, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-321: We first test the validity of $d_{ist}$, which yields a $P$-value of $0.326$, suggesting that $d_{ist}$ cannot be rejected as a valid instrumental variable.
elements.sent-2411-322: This is consistent with the findings of \cite{voors2012violent .}
elements.sent-2411-323: Similarly, testing $a_{lti}$ as a potential IV results in a $P$-value of $0.758$, indicating that $a_{lti}$ also cannot be rejected as a valid IV.
elements.sent-2411-324: These results align with the conclusions of \cite{voors2012violent .}
elements.sent-2411-325: In this paper, we explored the testability of single IVs in the additive nonlinear, non-constant effects (ANINCE) model, where the treatment variable can be either discrete or continuous.
elements.sent-2411-326: To this end, we introduced a necessary condition, termed the AIT Condition, to detect whether a variable is a valid IV without knowing whether some other variable is an instrument.
elements.sent-2411-327: Furthermore, we provided the precision conditions for identifying all invalid IVs in linear and ANINCE models.
elements.sent-2411-328: We then proposed the practical AIT condition test algorithm with covariates and finite data.
elements.sent-2411-329: Experimental results using both simulation data and three real datasets have further validated the usefulness of our algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-330: In the future, we plan to investigate whether the AIT condition could facilitate the testability implication of an invalid IV set.
